summary: Gruber responds to Dave Winer's speculation that Apple's Mac App Store would run actual iPad/iPhone apps on Mac hardware. He argues this won't happen because touchscreen direct manipulation is fundamentally different from mouse/trackpad input, pointing to the iOS Simulator as practical proof that iOS apps are awkward on Macs. While agreeing Apple will make Mac development more iOS-like, he predicts this will come through a Mac variant of UIKit rather than running iPad apps directly. He draws a parallel to how iPhone apps technically run on iPad but provide a poor experience, and concludes that Apple's philosophy is about device-specific optimization, not write-once-run-anywhere.
key_points: iOS apps already run on Macs via the developer simulator, and the experience proves they're awkward with mouse/trackpad input — touchscreen gestures don't translate well
The fundamental barrier isn't ARM vs. x86 or frameworks — it's that direct touch manipulation and cursor-based input are different interaction paradigms
Apple allowed iPhone apps on iPad only to ensure thousands of apps at launch, not because it was a good experience — the Mac doesn't have that bootstrap problem
A more plausible future is a Mac variant of UIKit that produces native Mac apps that are inherently more iOS-like, rather than running iPad apps directly
Apple's success philosophy is the opposite of 'write once, run anywhere' — it's about versions specifically optimized for each device
money_quotes: There's no doubt that iOS is where the developer mojo is. Not just Apple's developer mojo, but the industry's. It's the hottest platform in the world, period.
Direct manipulation on a touchscreen is fundamentally different than moving a mouse cursor via a touchpad.
Gestures that are natural and fun with direct touch are awkward and clumsy using a mouse or touchpad.
I never hear iPad developers — who run their own iOS apps on their Macs during development, for testing and debugging purposes — wish that they could ship them as-is to Mac users.
Everyone with an iPad knows that non-iPad-optimized iPhone apps stink on the iPad.
The whole point of Apple's success with iOS has been the opposite of 'write once, run anywhere'. It's more like 'write a version that is specifically optimized for this particular device'.
Can I imagine a future variant of UIKit for the Mac, which results in native Mac apps that are inherently more iOS-like? Yes.
key_insight: Apple's success comes from device-specific optimization rather than cross-platform universality, so iPad apps running unmodified on Macs would contradict the very philosophy that makes Apple's platforms great.
